system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — 1939-method

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

Chapter 1 establishes the biomechanical and conceptual foundation that every subsequent chapter builds upon. The method's first and most persistent commitment is to reduce guitar technique to explicit mechanical laws, each stated as a near-absolute rule with justification drawn from physics and anatomy rather than from feel or tradition. Posture, seated position, and guitar angle are specified so that the hands remain free of stabilizing duty. Right-hand mechanics center on pick oscillation (as opposed to bending the wrist), a wrist axis positioned over the highest note being played (the dynamic-axis rule), and a 2:1 hand-to-elbow speed ratio that keeps hand orientation consistent as the player traverses the neck. Left-hand mechanics are equally legislated: thumb one-third around the neck, fingers arched and suspended half an inch above the strings at all times, and the wrist held straight as an inviolable principle. Chapter 1 also introduces the Solfeggio orientation that runs through the entire method — every key's tonic is treated as "do," equalizing all twelve keys and preventing any single key from receiving privileged practice attention.

The method's core execution doctrines are introduced in Chapter 1 and enforced throughout Chapters 2 and 3. Attack-and-exit timing requires that the pick strike and fretting pressure land simultaneously, eliminating the common habit of fretting before or after picking. Legato execution is defined precisely: every note sustains its full value, and the next hand position is pre-formed during the motion between positions, not after arrival. Slow-practice tempo is a structural rule rather than a suggestion — all exercises are internalized at whatever pace permits perfect execution, and the method explicitly prohibits stopping mid-exercise regardless of how slow that tempo must be. String-set notation is established as the spatial vocabulary for describing where exercises live on the neck, and this vocabulary carries forward into the harmonized scale systems of Chapters 2 and 3.

Chapters 2 and 3 apply the Chapter 1 mechanics to harmonized scale studies — major triads in Chapter 2, minor triads in Chapter 3 — in each case organized into six distinct fingering forms. These six forms are not interchangeable shortcuts; the method requires that all six be practiced equally, in long, medium, and condensed variants across their assigned string sets, in order to build what Chapter 2 calls "balanced technique." The sequence form is the structural pattern for this work: three steps up and back, repeated through the harmonized scale. Chapter 2 introduces arpeggio picking as the standard right-hand approach for these studies, defined specifically as a pulsation technique in which downstrokes stop against the adjacent string rather than forcibly passing through, and Chapter 3 adds alternate picking as the prescription for second and fourth triads in minor contexts.

The left-hand vocabulary grows substantially across Chapters 2 and 3. Legato shift — quick, accurate position changes in which all notes receive equal value and no sliding occurs at modulation points — is introduced in Chapter 2 as a cardinal requirement for moving between forms. Finger flattening, described as the method's most advanced left-hand principle, requires a finger to sustain adjacent notes simultaneously during a barre or flat maneuver. Chapter 3 contributes the rocker-arm technique, an alternating motion between adjacent fingers used specifically to achieve legato on diminished triads without sliding. Finger suspension above strings — fingers hovering rather than resting — is stated as the means of preventing unintentional slides and ensuring clean voice separation throughout both chapters.

The method is also a theory-integrated technique curriculum. Harmonized scale studies are not purely physical exercises; they are the vehicle through which the player learns chord structure, voice leading, and key relationship simultaneously. Chapter 2's instruction to sustain inner voices while the melody moves encodes counterpoint awareness directly into the physical drill. Chapter 3 introduces contrary motion (voices moving in opposite directions) and exploits diminished symmetry — the property that diminished voicings repeat every four frets — as both a fingering economy and a music-theory observation. Open voicing and broken string sets in Chapter 3 extend the spatial vocabulary introduced in Chapter 1's string-set notation.

The pedagogical architecture across all three chapters follows a consistent master pattern: state the mechanical rule, name the error it prevents, specify the practice prescription, and then embed the exercise in a key-equalized transposition regimen via Solfeggio. Common mistakes are preempted by name — rushing the final note of each measure (Chapter 2), using conventional fingerings that produce slides where legato is required (Chapter 3), and forcing picks through strings rather than pulsing (Chapter 2). The etude composition method introduced in Chapter 3 — weekly construction of original studies under fixed fingering constraints — is the method's explicit prescription for internalizing its principles beyond the printed exercises. Taken together, the three chapters constitute a unified system in which posture and grip laws from Chapter 1 are the constant substrate, the six-form harmonized scale is the primary technical vehicle, and arpeggio picking, legato shift, finger flattening, and rocker-arm technique are the named moves through which that vehicle is mastered.

# Chapter 1 — General Instructions

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 establishes the foundational posture, hand mechanics, and practice principles that underpin all subsequent technique in the method. The core physical setup begins with a specific seated position and guitar angle that keeps the instrument stable and the hands free, followed by precise specifications for right-hand wrist position, pick grip (emphasizing oscillation rather than bending), and the signature 2:1 hand-to-elbow speed ratio that maintains consistent hand orientation across the neck. The left hand is governed by equally exacting rules: thumb placement one-third around the neck, arched fingers suspended half an inch above the strings, and a straight wrist maintained as an inviolable mechanical principle. The method introduces key load-bearing concepts including the Solfeggio system (treating each key's tonic as "do" to equalize all twelve keys), the dynamic-axis rule (wrist axis over the highest note for proper chord voicing), the attack-and-exit timing principle (simultaneous pick-strike and fretting-pressure), and the legato execution standard (full note values with pre-forming of the next position during hand motion). Throughout, the master's characteristic move is the reduction of guitar technique to explicit mechanical rules—each gesture stated as a near-absolute, with justifications drawn from biomechanics ("like machinery") and physics (oscillation, axis alignment)—and the definition of the string-set notation system and slow-practice tempo rule that structure how all exercises are to be learned.

# Chapter 2 — With Major Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces six distinct fingering forms for harmonized major scale triads, each covering specific key ranges and requiring separate practice as long, medium, and condensed forms across different string sets. The master's characteristic approach emphasizes foundational position systems and voice independence, as evidenced by the requirement to sustain inner voices while the melody moves and the explicit instruction to practice all three forms equally for balanced technique. Key load-bearing terminology includes arpeggio picking (defined as a pulsation technique using pick stops on downstrokes rather than forcibly striking strings), legato shift (quick accurate position changes with equal note values and no sliding at modulation points), finger flattening (the method's most advanced left-hand principle, requiring sustained adjacent notes during the maneuver), and sequence form (the three-steps-up-and-back structural pattern). The chapter establishes critical execution rules: maintaining even tempo across mixed note values, avoiding the common error of rushing the final note in each measure, ensuring clean octave crossings in condensed forms, and practicing barre variations with each finger in turn, all building toward the culminating goal of a seamless two-octave harmonized scale.

# Chapter 3 — With Minor Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 introduces harmonized minor scale studies in six fingering forms, establishing arpeggio picking and transposition to all keys as the standard treatment, alongside the constant goal of legato playing across all forms. The chapter's characteristic moves include precise left-hand rules—such as finger suspension above strings to prevent slides, the rocker-arm alternation for legato diminished triads, and deadening adjacent strings for open voicing—paired with specific right-hand prescriptions like the up-stroke arc around upper strings and alternate picking on second and fourth triads. Load-bearing terminology includes the harmonized minor scale, broken string sets, contrary motion, diminished symmetry (repeating every four frets), open voicing, rocker-arm technique, and the etude composition method (weekly construction with fixed fingering constraints). Throughout, the chapter preempts common mistakes by normalizing unconventional but correct fingerings and physical discomfort, while emphasizing that exercises yield benefit only when performed without stopping, no matter the tempo.

# Chapter 1 — General Instructions

_Pages 2-7._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Solfeggio / all-keys practice

> Think of the tonic of every key as “do”; this
> is called the Solfeggio system. Therefore if you are in E flat, consider the E flat as “do”. Through this
> system all keys are equal and therefore you will not favor any particular key or keys.

**Significance (p. 2).** This defines the method's core transposition rule, establishing how every exercise must be practiced across all twelve keys using the Solfeggio framework.

## Guitar posture / holding position

> Sit in a straight-
> back chair of medium height and
> then cross the left leg over the right
> so that your left knee rests on your
> right knee at the same time keeping
> your right foot flat on the floor.
> ‘Then place the body of the guitar
> on your lap so that the lower hollow
> fits the left leg and the upper hollow
> rests against the right side of your
> chest.

**Significance (p. 3).** This passage prescribes the exact seated posture required before any technique can be correctly executed, making it the physical foundation of the entire method.

## Guitar angle and scroll height

> The body
> of the guitar should be on an angle
> of approximately twenty degrees in relation to your torso and the scroll should be on a level with your
> shoulder, but slightly forward. Never lean back in the chair, lean slightly forward always, as this helps to
> hold the instrument securely.

**Significance (p. 3).** This specifies the precise angular and lean rules that keep the guitar balanced and the hands free, rules that must be internalized before picking or fretting technique is addressed.

## Right-hand / wrist position

> Bring the right arm up until the elbow rests very lightly on the top edge of the body. The end of
> your hand should now be half way between the bridge and the end of the fingerboard. The inside of
> the wrist should be approximately two inches above the string level. Fold the fingers of the right hand |
> under, but not so far as to have them touch the palm of the hand.

**Significance (p. 3).** This defines the precise arm, wrist, and finger geometry for the right hand, establishing the neutral position from which all picking strokes originate.

## Pick grip and oscillation

> Now place the pick on the first joint of the first finger so that the horizontal axis is parallel to the
> back of your hand. Then place the end of your thumb (approximately 3/4 inch) on the pick so that
> there is a half inch of the pointed end showing. Do not hold the pick too tightly as it must be allowed to
> oscillate rather than bend.

**Significance (p. 4).** This gives the exact pick-placement geometry and articulates the oscillation principle that governs tone production throughout the method.

## Wrist stroke and dynamic axis

> In a complete wrist action the wrist imitates a twisting motion with each stroke,
> very much like flicking something off your hand. See that you use a quick and accurate stroke, eliminating
> all excess movement because you want the notes to sound simultaneously, not one by one. When playing
> on inside strings use the next highest string as a pick-stop. The axis of your wrist should be directly over
> the highest note as the top note should predominate.

**Significance (p. 4).** This passage defines both the mechanics of the wrist stroke and the dynamic voicing rule (axis over highest note) that shapes chord balance across all the harmonic exercises in the book.

## Left-hand thumb position

> The thumb must ride approximately one-third of the way around the neck from the bass side, Never
> let your thumb extend above the fingerboard level, and never let it travel more than halfway around the
> curve of the neck. Your knuckles should be almost parallel to the side of the neck.

**Significance (p. 5).** These thumb-placement rules are stated as absolutes and govern the entire range of left-hand positions used in the method.

## Elbow-speed / hand-speed ratio

> Slide your hand slowly up the neck toward the body. When your hand touches the body of the instrument
> your elbow should be next to your torso. “Now slide along the neck slowly with your elbow traveling at
> just half the speed of your hand. This principle is similar to the hour and minute hand of a clock. If
> this is properly applied you will notice the position of the hand will change very little in relation to the
>
> neck, This is of great importance in the development of mechanical perfection.

**Significance (p. 5).** The 2:1 hand-to-elbow speed ratio is a signature technique of the method, keeping hand orientation constant relative to the neck across all positions.

## Finger arch and fret placement

> The fingers must be arched until just the tips rest on the strings so that they work up and down ham-
> mer-fashion seating just back of the frets, not in between and not on top.

**Significance (p. 5).** This is the foundational left-hand fretting rule, specifying arch shape, contact point, and fret placement that all subsequent exercises assume.

## Fingers suspended above strings

> It is necessary to keep the
> fingers suspended over the finger-
> board at all times. Do not let them
> stand up straight, curl under the
> fingerboard, or wander in any fash-
> ion. The correct place is approxi-
> mately one-half inch above the
> strings for in this position they are
> always ready to operate. This greatly
>
> improves accuracy of the fingers.

**Significance (p. 5).** The half-inch suspension rule is an explicit positioning standard that directly governs the accuracy and readiness of the left hand throughout all exercises.

## Straight left wrist rule

> The wrist must be kept straight at all times except when executing
> a very long reach. Your hand is like a piece of machinery which can develop mechanical trouble if thrown into
> odd positions. Those little push-rods in the back of the hand that operate the fingers must have a straight
> course if expected to work properly,

**Significance (p. 5).** The straight-wrist rule is stated as a near-absolute mechanical law, underpinning all left-hand technique in the method.

## Legato practice principle

> It isimportant to remember that the exercises in this book should be practiced very legato. In order
> to do so, the notes must be given their full value and must be connected with no pause between them. The
> changes from formation to. formation must be executed in the least amount of time. Do not stint the value
> of the notes in order to give yourself time to make the next formation. In making these quick shifts, do
> not rush the tempo. Plant your fingers solidly and firmly on the fingerboard. After releasing the pressure
> on a formation get used to forming the next position while the hand is in motion. Do not wait until the
>
> hand arrives at the location before forming the fingers.

**Significance (p. 6).** This passage defines the legato execution standard—the primary phrasing requirement for every exercise in the book—and gives the specific shift technique (pre-forming in motion) that makes it achievable.

## Attack and exit mechanics

> In the correct attack the pressure is applied the instant the pick strikes the string.
> Always apply the pressure quickly with a deliberate snap, like a trigger releasing a strong spring. In the
> exit of a note or chord, the pressure must be released as quickly as it was applied because a slow release pro-
> duces a bad buzzing sound, especially when working on the lower strings. The pressure release must be
> straight up off the fingerboard and not slanting in the direction of the next position, otherwise a slurring
> effect will be the result.

**Significance (p. 6).** This defines the synchronized attack-and-exit timing rule—simultaneous pick-strike and fretting pressure—that determines clean tone production in every chord exercise.

## String set / symbol system

> In this method the strings are listed in groups, or sets (see the chart), and for each set there is a symbol,
> either numerical, alphabetical, or both. This system has to be used because of the many different locations
> to play the same notations.

**Significance (p. 7).** This defines the string-set notation system that organizes every exercise in the book, making it essential for reading any subsequent chart or fingering diagram.

## Slow practice / tempo-building rule

> practice an exercise very slowly for a long time. This gives you time to think
> of all the points thus establishing the foundation for a good, clean technique. This can only be had by
> practicing slowly and gradually increasing the tempo, at the same time never practicing an exercise faster
>
> than you can:play it correctly.

**Significance (p. 7).** This is the method's core practice rule—never exceed correct tempo—which governs how all technical material in subsequent chapters should be approached.

# Chapter 2 — With Major Chords

_Pages 8-17._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## harmonized major scale fingerings

> The first exercise is a harmonized major scale in triads using six different fingerings. The first form is
>
> on the second and first sets of three strings.(See notations below the staff.) The second form of Ex. 1 is
> played entirely on the second set of three strings. The third form is played on the second and first sets of
> three strings but varies from the first form because the cross-over is on a different note of the scale. The
> fourth form is on the third, second and first sets of three strings. The notation in all the forms of the
> first exercise is the same but the fingerings are different in each one, and should be practiced as separate
> exercises. You will notice that the first four forms of Ex. 1 are long forms which cover quite a bit of the
> fingerboard. The fifth form is more condensed and the sixth form is the most condensed form of this harmo-
> nized scale. This first exercise is written in whole notes with no division of bars because the notes are of equal
> value and should be practiced very slowly.

**Significance (p. 8).** This passage establishes the method's foundational position system — six distinct fingering forms across string sets — which all subsequent exercises explicitly reference back to.

## key ranges per form

> Ist form—from C up to F
>
> 2nd form—from C up to C sharp (D if possible)
>
> 3rd form—from C up to F
>
> 4th form—from A flat up to D flat (D if possible)
>
> sth form—from A flat up to E
>
> 6th form—from F sharp up to C sharp

**Significance (p. 8).** This table of key ranges defines exactly how far each fingering form can be transposed, making it the practical roadmap for applying all six forms across the full guitar range.

## arpeggio picking definition

> Ex. No. 2 ss a preparatory exercise. This form of picking is termed arpeggio picking. To get ac-
>
> quainted with this form for the right hand, it is used on open strings so that you have to concentrate only
> on the pick and wrist action. It should be developed to sound even dynamically and steady rhythmically.

**Significance (p. 9).** This passage formally names and defines arpeggio picking as the method's core right-hand technique, isolating it on open strings before it is combined with left-hand work.

## pulsation picking mechanic

> To explain the pick and wrist action in Ex. 2 is difficult as we do not actually pick each string individually.
> The pick passes over each string and accents it with a slight kick, which is more of a pulsation. The reason
> for this is that using the pulsation principle you will be able to maintain a steady tempo and you will not
> strike two strings at once, as you might do if you were just forcibly pushing the pick across the strings. In
> other words it should have a smooth but deliberate effect. If and when you do strike two strings to-
> gether, you will know that you’re not “pulsating” properly, if at all. In the down strokes, use the next
> highest string as a pick stop. In the up strokes, you will not need a stop as the pick returns in in upward
> motion.

**Significance (p. 9).** This is the method's key technical description of how to execute arpeggio picking, introducing the pulsation concept, the pick-stop rule for downstrokes, and the diagnostic test for correct execution.

## legato shift and equal note value

> Practice this exercise in all its forms slowly. Also make sure that the notes
> are of equal value, as the tendency is to skip over the last note in each measure in order to get to the next
> position in time. Practice all these exercises as legato as possible, which will necessitate a quick accurate shift
>
> from one position to another.

**Significance (p. 9).** This passage articulates the method's two core execution rules — equal note value and legato playing — and directly identifies the most common student error (rushing the last note of a measure).

## sequence form definition

> In this exercise the major scale (harmonized in common triads) is shown in sequence form, three
> steps up and return on the first triad, then, on the second triad, etc., ascending; the reverse, descending.

**Significance (p. 10).** This defines the "sequence form" pattern — three steps up and back — which recurs as a structural device in multiple later exercises.

## even tempo with mixed note values

> Care must be taken in maintaining an even tempo, as every other measure is in arpeggio picking and
>
> the natural tendency is to hurry the whole notes. Count four beats for the first measure just as you
>
> would for the second measure.

**Significance (p. 11).** This identifies a specific rhythmic pitfall when mixing whole-note triads with arpeggio measures and prescribes the counting discipline to correct it.

## left-hand distance judgement

> This exercise develops judgement of distance with the left hand. Note that the gap between the first
> and the second triad is an octave which when played in the long form isalong jump. Care should be taken
> in making sure that the fingers light surely and firmly. In the condensed form you do not have the haz-
>
> ard of a long jump between first and second triads, but crossing over the sets of strings to jump the oc-
>
> tave presents a different problem as this must be done cleanly.

**Significance (p. 11).** This passage defines two distinct left-hand challenges — the long positional jump in extended forms versus the cross-string octave crossing in condensed forms — and names accuracy of landing as the critical skill.

## barre finger variations

> In the seventh measure of
>
> this exercise, the second triad should be played not only with a Barre of the fourth finger as marked, but
> also with Barres of the third, second and first fingers alternately.

**Significance (p. 12).** This is the method's explicit instruction to practice barre technique with each finger in turn, broadening the technique beyond just the first or fourth finger.

## two-octave scale assembly

> This exercise is in two sharps because that is the lowest form of the harmonized scale on the guitar.
> After all the forms have been perfected join them up with the harmonized scale an octave higher and
>
> practice without a break. You will then be able to run two octaves in the harmonized scale.

**Significance (p. 13).** This passage gives the method's explicit goal of linking lower and upper forms into a seamless two-octave harmonized scale, marking the culmination of the form-building work.

## finger flattening principle

> This exercise must be practiced very carefully as we introduce a new principle iin ‘the first two forms
> which is the “breaking” (or flattening from an arched position) of the first joint of the fingers. In
> the first form at the second and third measures, you flatten the first joint of the:second finger to produce
> the added note, which brings this principle into the classification of a fifth finger. It is a very difficult
> maneuver because the finger that is doing the flattening must sustain another note during the process. In
> the second form, in the first and last measures, the third finger does the flattening which is also very diffi-
> cult. This flattening principle must be practiced methodically as it must be reliable rhythmically and should
> be done with a snap. Do not skip over this principle as it is very important.

**Significance (p. 15).** This passage formally introduces and defines the finger-flattening technique — the method's most advanced left-hand principle in this chapter — explaining its mechanism, difficulty, and rhythmic requirement.

## modulation technique rule

> The same principles should be applied, making sure that the
> point of modulation is clean and distinct. Do not slide the left hand in the modulation. It should be a fast,
>
> accurate change. The pressure should be released for just a fraction of a second during the change.

**Significance (p. 15).** This gives a precise rule for executing position changes at modulation points — no sliding, a momentary pressure release — which is the method's standard for clean shifting.

## sustained inner voices rule

> The upper (me-
> lodic) line is in half notes while the two lower voices are in whole notes. Make sure they sustain their full
>
> value. Practice all three forms equally as the purpose throughout this method is balanced technique.

**Significance (p. 14).** This establishes the voice-independence requirement — inner voices must sustain while the melody moves — and states the method's overarching pedagogical goal of balanced technique.

# Chapter 3 — With Minor Chords

_Pages 18-42._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Harmonized minor scale study

> Starting a harmonized minor scale study in six different forms. Pay close attention to the markings
>
> in these forms as they are very confusing and difficult. Watch the movement of all the fingers closely.
>
> Give this exercise the same treatment as Ex. 1, such as arpeggio picking, etc. Practice in, all keys.

**Significance (p. 18).** This sets the governing practice method for the entire opening section of minor scale exercises, establishing arpeggio picking and all-keys transposition as the standard treatment.

## Legato practice principle

> Always bear in mind the legato practice and the use of the six different fingerings. Practice in all
>
> keys.

**Significance (p. 19).** This is the method's explicit rule that legato must be a constant goal across all six fingering forms, not just a stylistic option.

## Lower harmonized minor scale

> This is a lower form (in pitch) of the harmonized minor scale. Practice very carefully as some of
>
> the fingerings, though correct, are confusing. Practice this also with the arpeggio picking and in all keys.

**Significance (p. 20).** Explicitly warns the student that unconventional but correct fingerings exist, preempting the common mistake of correcting them away.

## Tonic-only harmonized minor

> The harmonized minor scale built on the tonic chord. To explain this more clearly in case of doubt, it is written in the key of D minor (using accidentals), using the tonic triad only, as harmony. Practice each form carefully as each fingering presents different hazards. This exercise is to be practiced in all keys.

**Significance (p. 22).** Defines a specific harmonic reduction technique — using only the tonic triad — and alerts the student that each fingering form carries its own technical danger.

## Seventh arpeggio fingerboard range

> Built on the seventh arpeggio which takes in the entire fingerboard. These exercises cannot be played
> in all the keys in their original form but can be moved up and down the fingerboard by leaving out the
> first measure when descending (if too low) and leaving out the fifth measure when ascending (if too high).

**Significance (p. 24).** Gives a specific structural rule for adapting full-fingerboard arpeggio exercises to avoid range limits, which is a practical transposition method unique to this style.

## Alternate picking / up-strokes

> Pay special attention to the markings as this exercise uses alternate picking, which means that
>
> the second and fourth triads in the measure are played with up-strokes.

**Significance (p. 25).** Defines alternate picking operationally within the context of triads, specifying exactly which beats take up-strokes — a foundational right-hand rule.

## Broken set of three strings

> This is the first of a series of stretching exercises. You will notice that the middle note remains the
> same while the other two voices move around the middle voice in chromatic tenths. This is the first exercise
> using a broken set of three strings. (See page 6). Pay close attention to the set markings under the staff
> in all chese exercises.. Practice in all keys in straight down strokes, then with arpeggio picking. Be careful
> in the latter 2s you have to jump over a string with the pick. This must be done smoothly.

**Significance (p. 26).** Introduces the concept of the broken string set as a distinct technical category and warns about the pick-jumping challenge that makes it harder than closed-set exercises.

## Contrary motion / hand gymnastics

> Contrary motion is introduced in this stretching exercise. This comes more under the heading of hand gymnastics. Constant practice of these two forms will definitely increase the spread of the fingers, particularly in the first form. Though dissonant in spots, it is correct. Practice in all keys which is made possible by the two forms.

**Significance (p. 27).** Categorizes contrary-motion exercises as "hand gymnastics" and reassures the student that resulting dissonances are structurally correct, preventing abandonment of a difficult exercise.

## Diminished chord repeats every four frets

> As you know the diminished chord repeats itself every four frets. Therefore it is necessary
> to have a good foundation in order to judge the distance between triads. You will notice the fingering re-
> mains the same throughout this exercise. The jumps should be fast and accurate with the fingers solidly im-
> planted each time. In other words, look out for slides. The first finger should rest just above the fourth
>
> string, not riding it, but suspended a little above the string. Practice slowly.

**Significance (p. 28).** States the defining property of diminished symmetry, then gives a precise left-hand rule — finger suspension above the string — to keep jumps clean and avoid unintentional slides.

## Idle finger suspension (reversed)

> The same principles apply in this exercise as in Ex. 48 except for the finger suspension, which is re-
> versed. This time the first, second and third fingers do the work, and the fourth finger is poised just above
> the first string. You may have to spend a lot of time on these first two diminished exercises to get the
> two idle fingers to lie in readiness. This is important and must be practiced until perfect.

**Significance (p. 28).** Establishes idle-finger readiness as a non-negotiable technique requiring dedicated practice until perfect, not merely a desirable habit.

## Rocker-arm finger alternation

> The first and fourth fingers should alternate like the
> ends of a rocker arm. In other words the first finger should remain down until the fourth comes down, but
> make sure there are never four notes sounding not even for a fraction of a second. With this fingering
> the exercise can be played very legato. Down strokes should be used throughout until thoroughly acquain-
> ted with the exercise, after which time alternate picking should be used, with the up-strokes falling on the
>
> second and fourth triads.

**Significance (p. 28).** Describes the rocker-arm technique as the mechanism for achieving legato in diminished triad sequences while simultaneously specifying the correct pick-stroke progression.

## Unusual second-finger fingering

> A rather unusual fingering is introduced in this exercise which will have to be practiced rationally as it
>
> Make sure that all the fingers except the second are arched. Don’t be alarmed if the first
>
> joint of the second finger aches slightly while it is learning to bear the strain. The fingering remains the
>
> same ascending until the octave is reached, then reverses and remains the same descending until the starting
>
> point is reached.

**Significance (p. 30).** Warns the student about physical discomfort from a non-standard finger position and normalizes the pain, preventing early abandonment of a technically important fingering.

## Two notes with one finger

> The chromatic diminished scale in this form is one of the most difficult exercises so far, inasmuch as you
> have to play two notes in succession with the first finger. The difficulty here is in making these two notes
> sound as though they were fingered with two fingers instead of one. This can be done by making the move-
>
> ment very fast without sliding or slurring. Practice slowly.

**Significance (p. 31).** Defines the specific technical challenge of playing two successive notes with one finger and gives the only solution — speed of movement — to avoid audible slides.

## Deadening a string (open voicing)

> This is the first diminished chord exercise in open voicing. It is necessary to *‘‘deaden” a string. This
> is taken care of by the fingering. For instance, in the first form the D string (IV) is stopped from vi-
> brating with the second finger while that finger is used for the note on the A (V) string. Practice slow-
> ly and observe all markings.

**Significance (p. 31).** Introduces the technique of using a fretting finger simultaneously to mute an adjacent string, a critical left-hand skill for open-voicing chord work.

## Up-stroke arc around upper string

> Make sure you do not
> deaden either of the sustaining voices with the up-stroke. This is accomplished as follows: After the down
> -stroke the pick should travel in a small returning arc around the upper string so you will not touch it
> and stop its vibration. Then with the descending backward motion the pick will strike the middle note
>
> safely. Use a complete wrist action.

**Significance (p. 32).** Gives a precise right-hand pick-path prescription — a small arc around the upper string — to isolate the middle voice in a three-voice texture, a technique with no obvious substitute.

## Hammer-down left-hand snap

> In other words, have the fingers
> formed before the two fingers drop and make sure they come down together firmly. Do not merely Jay them
> down but snap them down hammer-fashion simultaneously with the pick stroke. Give the notes their full
>
> value and practice slowly.

**Significance (p. 34).** Prescribes a specific left-hand attack — simultaneous hammer snap coordinated with the pick stroke — as a rule for clarity and rhythmic accuracy.

## Sustaining bass under moving fingers

> You may have diffi-
> culty in sustaining the bass note while the other fingers are performing. This is overcome by concen-
>
> tration and a little more pressure on that one note. Practice slowly.

**Significance (p. 35).** Identifies the common failure mode of bass-note drop-out and gives the specific remedy — increased pressure plus concentration — rather than a generic "practice more."

## Fourth finger arch rule

> This exercise increases the reach of the fourth finger. This finger will have a tendency to
>
> lay flat when it should be arched. This may be a strain at first but it can be developed through work.

**Significance (p. 30).** States the governing left-hand rule for the fourth finger and acknowledges physical resistance, framing correct arch as a developmental goal rather than an innate ability.

## Lower strings require more pressure

> More pressure has to be applied when playing on the lower strings in order to obtain a clear tone. Practice carefully.

**Significance (p. 37).** Establishes a physical rule about string tension and fretting pressure that applies across the entire method whenever exercises are transposed to lower string sets.

## Up-stroke revision drill

> Go back through the book now, taking all exercises in which down-strokes only were used and practice
>
> them using up-strokes. Then practice them using up and down strokes alternately. WARNING: In
>
> crossing the strings with the pick, make sure the up-strokes are played just as quickly as the down strokes.
>
> Try to make them sound alike.

**Significance (p. 39).** Issues a method-wide retrospective drill requiring up-stroke and alternate-stroke revision of all prior exercises, with an explicit warning that up-strokes must match down-stroke speed and tone.

## Etude composition method

> You should try writing one of these every week, applying the following rules. Limit yourself to a
> certain number of exercises (five or six for example), varying the value of the notes any way you wish
> without changing the fingering. You can use any part of the exercises selected, from the smallest part to
> the whole exercise. Try to vary each etude from the rest. Remember you can not milk any of: the ex-
> ercises dry, as there is always a new way to twist them around. Keep them as simple as possible at first, for
> if they become too complicated it will be difficult to resolve them properly.

**Significance (p. 40).** Lays out the method's compositional pedagogy — weekly etude construction with a fixed fingering constraint — as a structured creative practice integral to the method's approach.

## Benefit from uninterrupted practice

> Benefit is derived
>
> from an exercise only when you can go through it without stopping, no matter how slowly you have to go
>
> in order to do so.

**Significance (p. 38).** States a universal practice principle — continuity over speed — that governs how all exercises in the method should be approached.
